visual_checks: Identify any live audio content in synchronized media (live streams, webinars, live events)
Check if the live video player offers real-time captions (CC button or auto-caption indicator)
Look for CART (Communication Access Realtime Translation) or live captioning service indicators
If captions are visible, assess whether they appear in real-time with reasonable accuracy
Check if the live event page mentions captioning availability in its description
pass_conditions: All live synchronized media provides real-time captions
Live captions are reasonably accurate and appear with minimal delay
A captioning method is clearly provided (CART, automated with human correction, or equivalent)
Captions identify speakers in multi-speaker live events when not visually obvious
fail_conditions: Live synchronized media has no captioning option
Live captions are present but too inaccurate to be usable (garbled, missing large portions)
Captioning service is advertised but not actually functional during the live event
Live captions have extreme delay (more than several seconds) making them unusable
na_conditions: Page contains no live synchronized media
All media content is prerecorded (covered by 1.2.2 instead)
Page has live audio-only content but no synchronized video
ne_conditions: AI response could not be parsed
Live content is not currently active and cannot be tested
Cannot confirm whether the content is live or prerecorded
common_mistakes: Relying solely on auto-generated live captions without any human monitoring or correction
Providing captions only for the main speaker but not for audience questions or panel discussions
Caption feed that drops out intermittently during the live broadcast
No indication to users that live captions are available — users do not know to enable them

examples.pass: A live webinar uses a CART captioner providing real-time captions with speaker identification, accessible via a CC toggle in the video player
examples.fail: A live-streamed town hall meeting has no captioning whatsoever — participants who are deaf cannot follow the discussion
examples.partial: Live auto-captions are enabled but technical terminology and proper nouns are frequently mangled with no human correction

plain_meaning: Live audio in synchronized media (a live webcast, live stream, live webinar with speakers on camera) must include captions as it is broadcast. The intent is that deaf and hard-of-hearing users can follow live events in real time. Unlike 1.2.2, there is no exemption for media that is an alternative for text, because live events rarely are one.
scope_applies_to: Live synchronized media streams embedded on the page (live webinars, live lectures, live news, live events)
Embedded live players for platforms that broadcast video with live audio
Scheduled live webcasts promoted from the page and played in-page
Live question-and-answer sessions with spoken audio and video
scope_does_not_apply_to: Prerecorded synchronized media -- that belongs to SC 1.2.2
Live audio-only content with no video -- that belongs to SC 1.2.9 (AAA)
Video-only (silent) live content with no audio
Archived recordings of previously-live events (those become prerecorded and fall under 1.2.2)
Audio description for live video -- not required at any WCAG level for live content
Missing alt text on a thumbnail launching the stream -- that belongs to SC 1.1.1
Keyboard access to the live player -- that belongs to SC 2.1.1
borderline_calibration: Professionally produced live captions (human or accurate automatic) satisfy the rule.
If the page only advertises a future live event without embedding it, 1.2.4 is not yet applicable.
If the event is already ended and only the recording is present, evaluate under 1.2.2 instead.
Missing captions on an embedded live stream is a hard failure at medium+.
